dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5; 911 call for a person down at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me why you called 911?
Erm, we found him unconscious in the bathroom, he's been there for about two hours.
He appears unresponsive. Do you have any idea how long he remained that way?
I think he was unconscious when I found him.
Let me check his responsiveness. Can you open your eyes for me?
Eyes remain closed.
If you can hear me, squeeze my hand.
Slight withdrawal of the right hand noted with painful stimulus.
Airway is clear with no signs of obstruction. Do you note any sounds during breathing?
No sign of airway obstruction or sounds.
Breathing is adequate with chest rise and fall. I'll place him on the pulse ox.
Respiratory rate is around 12 per minute.
SpO2 is 84 on ambient air.
Assessing pulse and skin condition.
Pulse is 73, feeling rapid and weak. Skin is diaphoretic.
Blood pressure reads 116 over 73.
Glucose level is 95 mg/dL. Logging now.
Do you know of any medical conditions or prior issues?
He lives alone, I don't know his medical history.
Has he taken any medications?
No medications were found with him. Not sure when he last ate.
Administering 2 mg Narcan nasally. Partner, get the oxygen ready.
Oxygen at 10 liters per minute via NRB is on.
Moderate response to Narcan noted, but pulse has increased to 157-monitor closely for perfusion concerns.
Blood pressure is 117/58.
Respiratory rate is 14 with adequate chest rise.
Placing patient on a 4-lead ECG to monitor cardiac status.
Transport required due to emergent acuity and partial response to treatment.
Patient is heading to [COMPANY_NAME]-Health Systems Cold immediately due to suspected overdose.
Ready to move the patient during transport.
Respirations have increased to 20 with adequate depth observed.
Blood pressure is recorded at 108/59, and pulse remains elevated at 118.
Full report has been provided to receiving staff in eDTA Room 11.
The patient was unresponsive upon initial contact and showed no pain response during the assessment.
No visible petechiae, rashes, or visible bites or stings on the skin.
No signs of lymphadenopathy or neck masses observed.
No drainage, hemorrhage, or foreign body in oral, nasal, or ear regions.
Adequate chest expansion and no adventitious breath sounds.
No murmurs noted and peripheral pulses remain weak but present.
Abdomen is flat with no noted rigidity or tenderness on palpation.
No rectal bleeding observed. Patient has not voided.
No visible deformities or signs of trauma to extremities or back.
4-lead ECG monitoring initiated per protocol.
Remain vigilant on BP and perfusion during transport, it's at the edge of normal ranges.
No mention of prior addiction or substance use history provided from bystander.
Bystander unsure but noted he was isolated in bathroom for two hours before found.
Sinus rhythm initially per EKG readings.
SpO2 remains at 84, will continue to re-monitor hourly if stable.
One dose of Narcan administered with moderate clinical response noted.
No signs of adverse effects to Narcan noted post-administration.
[COMPANY_NAME] is primary choice due to cardiac response and suspected opioid involvement.
Continue to monitor airway patency and perfusion status during transport.
Patient still reports no pain, pain assessment recorded at zero.
Bystander unsure but noted he was isolated in bathroom for two hours before found.
Head is secured during transfer and placed on stretcher with draw sheet.
Diaphoresis still present, no signs of dehydration or hypothermia.
Pupils appear equal and round. Still no pupil response observed.
All equipment secured and patient is ready for transport.
Trending toward sinus rhythm, no acute inferior or posterior ST changes observed.
No acute distress observed post-reassessment during initial transport phase.
I saw something on the floor I think was related to overdose accessories. Not sure exactly what.
All parameters are logged and equipment is functioning within normal limits.
All extremity pulses remain equal and capillary refill within normal limits.
Patient's airway, breathing, and circulation are all currently stable for transport.
No use of accessory muscles, no signs of stroke noted.
No developing tachycardia observed as of now.
No hold-ups at hospital, receiving staff confirmed rapid DDx setup.
At this point, no critical changes in vital signs or neurological status.
Patient awake enough to blink periodically but not interactive yet in conversation.
I heard a crash, then went in and found him face down on the bathroom floor.
No prior use of medical services or documentation from previous days available.
All IV sites were attempted but remain non-functional for fluids or meds during transport.
Pulse Ox is requested to be rechecked at every 10 minutes until perfusion stabilized.
Attempting to get baseline renal function with available history.
Heart rate is noted but nothing globally concerning at this phase.
No immediate interventions expected during transport beyond current protocols.
Securing the patient with restraints as per policy.
No abnormalities on palpation of abdomen.
Proceeding to transport as per emergency code red status.
No additional interventions requested by receiving facility.
All steps captured for detailed ED handoff once on scene.
Noting improved response but still decreased mental status.
SpO2 and Respirations improve with repositioning, better oxygen perfusion assumed.
Not appreciating bradycardia at this moment.
Transport time is fast, protocol allows for no delay.
No modifications in transfers or lifts planned-will follow standard protocol.
Arterial pressure seen trending in better range with limited fluctuation.
Patient awake enough to follow basic commands for transport.
No further inconsistencies in cardiovascular functioning.
All necessary interventions documented and ready for ED handoff.
No temperature awareness sensation observed.
Transport designated as primary emergent based on clinical impression.
No prior overdose history, possible new or assumed drug user.
[COMPANY_NAME]-Cold is currently best bet for early rapid response.
Patient restraint readjusted for comfort during long transport.
No acute agitation or challenging behaviors for transport.
Patient stabilized for movement into ambulance.
Pulses taken at different sites: radial is weak, pedal intact.
Clean chest surface, no jewelry and skin tape in place.
Spinal precautions not needed due to unresponsive state and no trauma indicators.
Alerting ED preparations that medical report will be iodinated.
Paramedic at front seat with cardiac monitor in use for trip.
Ready for two-minute redraw or defibrillation if changes.
Transportation of this patient is clear with known route.
Transport vitals remaining within the standard limits we established.
Prepare to confirm projected arrival time with dispatch.
Pulse trending toward normal limits during leg of trip.
Safe carry and secure transfer to ED with nurse backup.
Chain of communication and information is closed and working.
Patient considered hospital ready with IV in attempt only.
No special handling or trauma cases in sim right now.
Patient care plan fully documented in roll call.
Patient alert level kept minimal but in dynamic range.
No further complications anticipated at this moment.
Transport successfully concluded with timely to ED.
